topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, the subject line a mocking reminder: "Congratulations on your publication!" My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. My name wasn't there. Instead, a stranger’s name, a name I didn't recognize, was emblazoned above the headline of the piece I had poured my soul into. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving a hollow ache in my chest. I reread the text, each sentence a fresh stab of betrayal. I felt the blood drain from my face, leaving a clammy sheen on my skin. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor, and walked to the window, staring blankly at the indifferent city below.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. The notification confirmed it. The article, my meticulously crafted narrative, was credited to an entirely different author. A profound sense of disorientation settled over me, making the air feel thick and hard to draw in. My hands began to tremble, the fine motor control failing me as I tried to process the sheer audacity of it. I leaned back in my worn office chair, the leather groaning in protest, and let out a sound that was more of a choked sigh than an actual word. Every muscle in my jaw tightened, pulling my lips into a thin, painful line. I needed to move, to disrupt this suffocating stillness, but my legs felt weighted, as if submerged in deep water.

[story 3]

When I finally saw the attribution, the color seemed to leach out of the room. It wasn't just a factual error; it felt like a deliberate erasure. A bitter taste flooded my mouth, metallic and sharp. I picked up the printed proof, the paper feeling suddenly flimsy and insulting in my hands. My fingers curled around the edges until the paper threatened to tear. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, something raw and uncontrolled. I dropped the paper onto the desk with unnecessary force, the sound echoing in the quiet space. I didn't bother to check my phone or reply to the notification; all I could do was pace the small perimeter of my room, the rhythm of my steps uneven and agitated, a caged animal searching for an exit.
